how-it-works.title: How This Works
content: Welcome to Tech Journey.
This is a story about four learners whose lives begin to change when they discover the hidden world behind computers, code, data, and artificial intelligence.
Read the chapter text aloud in the upper reading section.
Tap purple words to open glossary meanings.
Use the recorder to record your reading, then play it back, clear it, or download it.
Complete the worksheet after each chapter.
As the story continues, you will not only follow the learners' journey but also discover important ideas about technology and the future.

code.definition: A set of instructions written for a computer to follow.
code.example: A program runs because of code.

artificial intelligence.definition: Technology that helps computers perform tasks such as analysing information, finding patterns, making predictions, or generating responses.
artificial intelligence.simpleMeaning: Smart computer systems that can help with many tasks.
artificial intelligence.example: Artificial intelligence can help sort information quickly.

worksheet.title: Getting Ready
worksheet.prompt: What do you think this story will teach you about technology, code, and the future?

ch1.title: Chapter 1: The Day the Screens Went Dark
content: The school computer lab was never silent.
Even when no one was speaking, the room made its own sounds.
Old desktop fans hummed. Loose cables brushed against the sides of desks. The fluorescent tube in the corner buzzed like an irritated insect.
Naledi had noticed all of that before she noticed the screens.
Every monitor in the lab was dark.
Not asleep. Not loading. Dead.
Ayaan was the first to say it out loud.
"That is not normal."
Kabelo leaned back in his chair and folded his arms. "Brilliant observation."
Thando pressed a key on the nearest keyboard. Nothing happened.
Naledi stood still near the doorway, her school bag hanging from one shoulder. Something about the room felt wrong in a way she could not yet explain.
The lab was usually one of the few places in school that felt connected to a bigger world.
When the machines were on, the room seemed full of possibility.
Now it felt sealed off.
Mr. Dlamini entered carrying a dented metal toolbox in one hand and a stack of old exercise books under his arm.
He looked at the blank screens, then at the four learners standing in different corners of the room.
"Good," he said.
Kabelo frowned. "Good?"
"Yes," said Mr. Dlamini. "Now you can stop trusting machines for five minutes and start asking questions."
That was the sort of thing he said often.
Teachers in other classrooms gave answers too quickly.
Mr. Dlamini preferred discomfort.
He placed the toolbox on the teacher’s desk and pulled out a small [[flash drive]].
"Every system runs on [[code]]," he said. "Code is just language. And language can be learned."
He plugged the flash drive into the main machine.
The screen stayed black.
"But learning it," he continued, "means understanding something most people never see."
Naledi folded her arms. "And what’s that?"
"That the world you think is automatic… isn’t."
Outside, a taxi hooted. Somewhere nearby, someone was arguing. The ordinary sounds of the afternoon drifted in through the broken window.
Ordinary.
Predictable.
Limited.
Naledi thought about her older brother. He had finished school two years ago. He was still 'looking for something.' Every week he said something new would happen. Every week nothing did.
She thought about the word [[future]].
Teachers used it like a promise.
But it felt more like a rumor.
"Sir," Thando said carefully, "people who do this… they work overseas, right?"
"Some do," Mr. Dlamini said.
"Not here," Thando replied.
Mr. Dlamini did not answer immediately.
"Technology doesn’t belong to a country," he finally said. "It belongs to whoever understands it."
The sentence hung in the air.
Belongs to whoever understands it.
Not whoever was born somewhere else.
Not whoever had the best accent.
Not whoever had money.
Whoever understands it.
Ayaan stared at the dead screen.
"Why did it crash?" he asked.
Mr. Dlamini smiled slightly. "Because someone updated something without checking the [[dependencies]]."
"Dependencies?" Kabelo frowned.
"Think of it like this," Mr. Dlamini said. "Every system relies on other systems. If one changes without telling the others, everything collapses."
Naledi felt that sentence more than she understood it.
Every system relies on other systems.
Families.
Schools.
Communities.
People.
"So you’re saying," Thando said slowly, "that one mistake can shut everything down?"
"Yes."
"That’s unfair."
Mr. Dlamini shrugged gently. "Computers don’t care about fair. They care about [[precision]]."
That word landed heavily.
Precision.
Life in their world wasn’t precise. It was messy. Loud. Uncertain.
But here, in this lab, there was something different.
If you understood the rules, you could control the outcome.
Mr. Dlamini stepped back from the machine.
"I can fix this," he said. "In twenty minutes."
Their shoulders relaxed.
"But that won’t change anything."
They looked at him.
"If I fix it," he continued, "you’ll go back to using the computers the way you always have."
He paused.
"Or I can show you why it broke."
Silence.
The kind that feels like a doorway.
Naledi felt something shift inside her.
She did not know exactly what [[programming]] was. She did not understand servers or systems.
But she understood this:
There was a world operating around her.
Invisible decisions.
Invisible instructions.
Invisible power.
And she had never questioned who wrote those instructions.
"Sir," she said quietly, "who wrote the code for this school system?"
"A company in Johannesburg."
"And before that?"
"Another company."
"And before that?"
"Someone else."
Naledi swallowed.
"So we just… use things other people decide for us?"
That question was no longer about computers.
Mr. Dlamini saw it.
"Yes," he said gently. "Unless you decide to become the person who writes the decisions."
Outside, the final bell rang.
Learners flooded past the lab windows.
The day was ending.
But inside that dim computer lab, something was beginning.
Not just curiosity.
Not just interest.
A quiet defiance.
Thando broke the silence first.
"If we learn this," he said, "can we build something ourselves?"
"Yes."
"Anything?"
Mr. Dlamini smiled.
"Anything that follows [[logic]]."
Kabelo leaned back in his chair. "What if we don’t know the logic?"
"Then," Mr. Dlamini said, "you discover it."
Naledi stared at the blank screen again.
For years she had thought technology was something that arrived — finished, complete, untouchable.
Now she saw it differently.
Not as a product.
As a language.
And language could be spoken.
Could be rewritten.
Could be owned.
"Don’t fix it," she said suddenly.
Everyone looked at her.
"What?"
She met Mr. Dlamini’s eyes.
"Don’t fix it for us."
A slow smile spread across his face.
"Good," he said.
And for the first time, the darkness of the screens did not feel like failure.
It felt like invitation.

flash drive.definition: A small storage device used to save and move digital files.
flash drive.simpleMeaning: A small device that carries computer files.
flash drive.example: Mr. Dlamini pulled out a flash drive from his toolbox.

code.definition: A set of instructions written for a computer to follow.
code.example: The system runs because of code.

future.definition: The time that is still to come.
future.example: Naledi wondered what her future would look like.

dependencies.definition: Parts of a system that rely on other parts in order to work properly.
dependencies.example: If dependencies break, the whole system can fail.

programming.definition: The process of writing instructions that tell computers what to do.
programming.example: Naledi did not yet understand programming.

logic.definition: A clear system of rules and reasoning that helps things make sense.
logic.simpleMeaning: The rules of how something works.
logic.example: A computer program must follow logic.

worksheet.title: The Beginning of the Journey
worksheet.prompt:
1. Why did the dark computer screens feel important to Naledi?

2. What did Mr. Dlamini mean when he said technology belongs to whoever understands it?

3. Why do you think Naledi said, 'Don’t fix it for us'?
